text: Here we present a video of robotic ampulectomy for a peri-ampullary neoplasm.
Our patient is a fifty nine year old female who was evaluated for upper abdominal pain of four months duration which was associated with loss of appetite and non quantified weight loss.
Ultrasound of abdomen showed a dilated CVD with soft tissue mass in the distal one-third. The blood investigations were normal.

MRCP showed a peri-emplary neoplasm with dilatation of both the bile and the pancreatic ducts. PET-CT scan showed a hypermetabolic soft tissue density in the peri-emplary region with ductal dilatation and no FDG-avid lymph nodes.
While the above investigations were pointing towards malignant etiology, interestingly, biopsy showed tumor with low to high-grade dysplasia.
So after counseling, the patient was planned for robotic amplectomy with frozen section biopsy and V-pulse procedure if indicated.
The surgery was performed in DaVinci X robo.
The surgical team places ports on the patient's abdomen, positioning the patient in reverse Trendelenburg to facilitate the robotic procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the gastrocolic ligament to achieve inferior mobilization of the transverse colon.
Placing the patient in reverse Tenderlenburg position helps in easy lowering and adequate exposure.
The surgeon frees the omental adhesions to the gallbladder and liver, continuing the dissection laterally until the hepatic flexure is completely mobilized.
The surgeon proceeds with the caucarization process, utilizing the DaVinci X robotic system to manipulate tissues within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses prograsp forceps to retract the duodenum upwards, carefully dissecting its lateral attachments away from the inferior vena cava. Robotic instruments facilitate precise dissection in the limited space.

The surgeon localizes the ampulla, identifying the large growth through tactile feedback. The use of intraoperative ultrasound and endoscopy is mentioned as alternatives for smaller tumors, while a preoperative stent serves as a guide in cases of obstructive jaundice.

The surgeon performs a duodenotomy on the lateral wall of the duodenum using an ultrasonic energy device, extending the incision longitudinally with hook electrocautery. The incision is made at the level of the ampulla to avoid an unnecessarily long duodenotomy.
The surgeon pulls the ampulla out through the duodenotomy. Stay sutures are placed over the duodenotomy to splay the duodenal wall open and provide retraction.

Stay sutures are placed over the ampulla, and ampullary transduction begins in the inframedial aspect using an ultrasonic energy device. The dissection deepens, revealing pancreatic juice flowing from a dilated pancreatic duct. A stitch is placed between the pancreatic duct and duodenal mucosa to prevent retraction and aid traction. The dissection continues superiorly, with bile flow visible from the bile duct.
The surgeon displays a dilated bile duct and pancreatic duct. The specimen is placed into an endo bag and removed for frozen section biopsy.

As the biopsy was negative for high-grade dysplasia or invasive malignancy, we proceeded with the ampulectomy alone. However, it is imperative to discuss the shortcomings of the ampulectomy with the patient and patient's family and the need for pancreaticodiurectomy if the biopsy report is positive.

The surgeon begins the reconstruction of the common bile duct (CBD) by suturing the superior aspect of the CBD to the duodenal mucosa using PDS-C-C sutures, suitable for the dilated ducts. A bridging anastomosis is performed between the CBD and the pancreatic duct.

They can be treated with endoscopic papillotomy, transduodenal ampullectomy, or pancreatic duodenectomy. However, compared to endoscopic papillotomy, transduodenal ampullectomy offers higher chance of complete resection. The ductal reconstruction is completed by taking interrupted sutures all around.
Robotic transduodenal ampullectomy provides the benefit of minimally invasive surgery for a procedure which would otherwise require a big wound to access a small lesion.
Stay sutures are placed over the duodenum to facilitate the closure of the duodenotomy using a blue EndoGIS stapler.
Duodenotomy can also be closed using sutures alone. While some surgeons do close duodenotomy vertically, it is preferable to close it transversely in order to avoid stricture formation.
The surgeon places a drain in the right subhepatic space and positions the omentum over the duodenotomy site, completing the procedure.
Patient was started on oral chips on post-op day two. The drain was removed on day five and patient was discharged on day seven.

The final histopathology report was intra-ampullary papillary tubular neoplasm with low-grade dysplasia with negative margins. These are rare neoplasms of the ampulla of Werther. They are biologically indolent but can follow adenoma carcinoma sequence. Even though invasive lesions follow malignant course, they have better prognosis than typical ampullary carcinoma.
Thank you for watching the video.
